title: OS Course Labs (Projects 1–6, RISC-V Bare-Metal OS)
summary: An operating systems lab that incrementally builds a bare-metal OS on a dual-core RV64IMAFDC SiFive Rocket processor across six projects (seven directories). Starts with a host-side bootloader image tool and a cooperative kernel, then evolves through preemptive scheduling with synchronisation primitives, process management with IPC, virtual memory with demand paging, a network device driver, and a full on-disk file system. Each later project directory carries forward and extends the scheduler and memory subsystems of the previous one.
instructionPreview: This is an operating-systems course lab (Projects 1–6) that implements a RISC-V bare-metal operating system. The repo spans six incremental projects: a bootloader, a simple cooperative kernel, a preemptive kernel with SMP, an interactive OS with process management and IPC, a virtual-memory-enabled kernel with demand paging, a device-driver layer with network support, and a file system. Entire subsystems have been removed and must be re-implemented.
instruction:
This is an operating-systems course lab (Projects 1–6) that implements a RISC-V bare-metal operating system. The repo spans six incremental projects: a bootloader, a simple cooperative kernel, a preemptive kernel with SMP, an interactive OS with process management and IPC, a virtual-memory-enabled kernel with demand paging, a device-driver layer with network support, and a file system. Entire subsystems have been removed and must be re-implemented.

Your working directory is `/workspace`. The project code is already present in seven subdirectories: `Project1-Bootloader`, `Project2-A_Simple_Kernel_Part1`, `Project2-A_Simple_Kernel_Part2`, `Project3-Interactive_OS_and_Process_Management`, `Project4-Virtual_Memory_Management`, `Project5-Device_Driver`, and `Project6-File_System`.

## Modules to Implement

The following modules need to be implemented or substantially built out. All stubbed function bodies contain `/* TODO: implement */`.

- **Bootloader Image Builder** (`Project1-Bootloader/createimage.c`): The host-side tool that reads ELF binaries and writes a bootable disk image. Two functions handle segment writing and OS size recording. (~1 file, 2 functions to implement)

- **Part 1 Scheduler & Locking** (`Project2-A_Simple_Kernel_Part1/kernel/sched/sched.c`, `kernel/locking/lock.c`): The cooperative round-robin scheduler, task blocking/unblocking primitives, and mutex lock acquire/release. (~2 files, 5 functions to implement)

- **Part 2 Scheduler & Synchronisation** (`Project2-A_Simple_Kernel_Part2/kernel/`): The preemptive priority-based scheduler with ready-queue management, priority-based task selection, timer expiration checking, mutex locking, binary semaphore operations, and futex wait/wakeup with hash-based node management. (~5 files, 9 functions to implement)

- **Process Management & IPC** (`Project3-Interactive_OS_and_Process_Management/kernel/`): Multi-core scheduler with process lifecycle management (spawn, exit, kill, waitpid), priority-based scheduling with CPU affinity masks, binary semaphore tracking, and a mailbox-based inter-process communication system for sending and receiving messages with blocking semantics. (~4 files, 12 functions to implement)

- **Virtual Memory Management** (`Project4-Virtual_Memory_Management/kernel/mm/mm.c`, `kernel/sched/sched.c`): SV39 three-level page table initialisation and teardown, user page allocation with NRU page replacement, swap-to-disk eviction, demand-paging page fault handling, shared memory mapping/unmapping, process creation/execution with ELF loading, and process termination with resource cleanup. (~2 files, 13 functions to implement)

- **Device Driver Layer** (`Project5-Device_Driver/drivers/net.c`, `kernel/mm/mm.c`, `kernel/sched/sched.c`): Network packet send/receive with poll and interrupt modes, IRQ mode switching, plus the full virtual memory and scheduler subsystems carried forward from Project 4 with additional page-helper functionality. (~3 files, 18 functions to implement)

- **File System** (`Project6-File_System/kernel/fs/`): A complete on-disk file system with superblock/bitmap-based allocation, inode and block management (alloc, free), directory operations (mkdir, rmdir, ls, cd), hard/soft linking, file operations (touch, cat, fopen, fread, fwrite), plus the full scheduler and virtual memory subsystems from Project 5. (~6 files, 24 functions to implement)

## Test-Coupled Signatures

The following functions are called or inspected directly by the test suite. You must implement them with these exact signatures:

- `static void write_segment(Elf64_Phdr phdr, FILE *fp, FILE *img, int *nbytes)` — writes an ELF segment to a disk image with 512-byte alignment
- `static void write_os_size(int nbytes, FILE *img)` — writes the OS sector count at offset 508 in the image
- `void do_scheduler(void)` (P2A) — cooperative round-robin scheduler
- `void do_block(list_node_t *pcb_node, list_head *queue)` (P2A) — moves a PCB to a block queue
- `void do_unblock(list_node_t *pcb_node)` (P2A) — moves a PCB back to the ready queue
- `void do_mutex_lock_acquire(mutex_lock_t *lock)` (P2A, P2B) — acquires a mutex with blocking
- `void do_mutex_lock_release(mutex_lock_t *lock)` (P2A, P2B) — releases a mutex and wakes waiters
- `void scheduler(void)` (P2B, P3, P4, P5, P6) — preemptive priority-based scheduler
- `list_node_t* max_priority_node(void)` (P2B, P3, P4, P5, P6) — selects the highest-priority ready task
- `pid_t do_spawn(task_info_t *task, void *arg, spawn_mode_t mode)` (P3) — creates a new process
- `void do_exit()` (P3, P4, P5, P6) — handles process exit and resource cleanup
- `int do_kill(pid_t pid)` (P3, P4, P5, P6) — terminates a process by PID
- `int do_waitpid(pid_t pid, ...)` (P3, P4, P5, P6) — waits for a child process to exit
- `ptr_t alloc_user_page(uint8_t pin, PTE *pgtable)` (P4, P5, P6) — allocates a physical page for user space
- `ptr_t selete_page(void)` (P4, P5, P6) — NRU page replacement to select a victim page
- `void write_to_sd(ptr_t pa)` (P4, P5, P6) — evicts a page to disk swap space
- `PTE* init_page_table(void)` (P4, P5, P6) — creates a three-level SV39 page table for a new process
- `void free_process_user_page(PTE *pgdir)` (P4, P5, P6) — recursively frees all pages owned by a process
- `uintptr_t shm_page_get(int key)` (P4, P5, P6) — maps a shared memory page into the current process
- `void shm_page_dt(uintptr_t addr)` (P4, P5, P6) — unmaps a shared memory page
- `void handle_page_fault(regs_context_t *regs, uint64_t stval, uint64_t cause)` (P4, P5, P6) — demand-paging page fault handler
- `long do_net_recv(uintptr_t addr, size_t length, int num_packet, size_t *frLength)` (P5) — receives network packets
- `void do_net_send(uintptr_t addr, size_t length)` (P5) — sends a network packet
- `void do_net_irq_mode(int mode)` (P5) — switches between poll and interrupt modes
- `uint16_t alloc_inode()` (P6) — allocates a free inode from the bitmap
- `uint32_t alloc_block()` (P6) — allocates a free block from the bitmap
- `void free_inode(uint16_t inode_id)` (P6) — frees an inode in the bitmap
- `void free_block(uint32_t block_id)` (P6) — frees a block in the bitmap
- `int mkfs(int print)` (P6) — initialises the file system superblock and root directory
- `int do_mkdir(char *dirname)` (P6) — creates a new directory
- `int do_rmdir(char *dirname)` (P6) — removes a directory
- `int do_ls(char *dirname, int mode, int *print_location_y)` (P6) — lists directory contents
- `int do_cd(char *dirname)` (P6) — changes the current working directory
- `int do_link(char *filename, char *newfile, int mode)` (P6) — creates hard or soft links
- `int do_touch(char *filename)` (P6) — creates a new empty file
- `int do_cat(char *filename, char *buffer)` (P6) — reads file contents into a buffer
- `int do_fopen(char *filename, int access)` (P6) — opens a file and returns a file descriptor
- `int do_fread(int fd, char *buffer, int size)` (P6) — reads data from an open file
- `int do_fwrite(int fd, char *buffer, int size)` (P6) — writes data to an open file
- `int do_mbox_open(char *name)` (P3) — opens or creates a named mailbox
- `int do_mbox_send(int mailbox_id, void *msg, int msg_length)` (P3) — sends a message to a mailbox
- `int do_mbox_recv(int mailbox_id, void *msg, int msg_length)` (P3) — receives a message from a mailbox
- `void binsem_op(int binsem_id, int op)` (P2B, P3) — performs lock/unlock on a binary semaphore
- `futex_node_t* get_node(volatile uint64_t *val_addr, int create)` (P2B) — looks up or creates a futex node
- `void futex_wait(volatile uint64_t *val_addr, uint64_t val)` (P2B) — blocks on a futex if value matches
- `void futex_wakeup(volatile uint64_t *val_addr, int num_wakeup)` (P2B) — wakes tasks blocked on a futex
- `void timer_check(void)` (P2B) — checks expired timers and invokes callbacks
- `pid_t do_exec(const char *file_name, int argc, char *argv[], spawn_mode_t mode)` (P4, P5, P6) — loads and executes an ELF binary as a new process
- `uintptr_t alloc_page_helper(uintptr_t va, uintptr_t pgdir)` (P5, P6) — allocates a page and maps it at a virtual address

moduleDag.project: OS Course Labs (Projects 1–6, RISC-V Bare-Metal OS)
moduleDag.description: An operating systems lab that incrementally builds a bare-metal OS on a dual-core RV64IMAFDC SiFive Rocket processor across six projects (seven directories). Starts with a host-side bootloader image tool and a cooperative kernel, then evolves through preemptive scheduling with synchronisation primitives, process management with IPC, virtual memory with demand paging, a network device driver, and a full on-disk file system. Each later project directory carries forward and extends the scheduler and memory subsystems of the previous one.

nodes.label: Project 1 — Bootloader Image Builder
nodes.description: Host-side ELF-to-disk-image tool. Implements write_segment (copies ELF loadable segments into a 512-byte-aligned disk image) and write_os_size (records the total OS sector count at byte offset 508 so the on-board bootblock knows how many sectors to load). Entirely independent of the kernel projects; each later project ships its own pre-filled copy of this tool.

nodes.label: Project 2A — Cooperative Scheduler & Mutex
nodes.description: First bare-metal kernel. Implements a cooperative round-robin scheduler (do_scheduler), explicit PCB blocking and unblocking primitives (do_block, do_unblock), and a blocking mutex with acquire/release (do_mutex_lock_acquire, do_mutex_lock_release). Establishes the PCB ready-queue design that all subsequent projects extend.

nodes.label: Project 2B — Preemptive Scheduler & Synchronisation
nodes.description: Extends Part 1 into a preemptive priority-based scheduler (scheduler, max_priority_node), timer expiration checking (timer_check), binary semaphore operations (binsem_op), and futex wait/wakeup with hash-bucketed node management (get_node, futex_wait, futex_wakeup). The sched.c and lock.c are more complex versions of P2A's files requiring familiarity with the cooperative design first.

nodes.label: Project 3 — Process Management & IPC Mailbox
nodes.description: Adds full process lifecycle management on the preemptive scheduler: do_spawn (PCB + stack allocation, context init), do_exit (resource cleanup), do_kill (signal-based termination), do_waitpid (blocking wait with child reaping), and CPU affinity masks for dual-core scheduling. Also implements a named mailbox IPC system (do_mbox_open, do_mbox_send, do_mbox_recv) with blocking semantics and per-process binary semaphore tracking.

nodes.label: Project 4 — Virtual Memory Management
nodes.description: Adds SV39 three-level page table management (init_page_table, free_process_user_page), NRU page replacement (selete_page), swap-to-disk eviction (write_to_sd), demand-paging page fault handler (handle_page_fault), user page allocation (alloc_user_page), shared memory mapping (shm_page_get, shm_page_dt), and ELF binary loading (do_exec). The sched.c evolves P3's process management with virtual address space integration.

nodes.label: Project 5 — Device Driver Layer (Network)
nodes.description: Adds a GigabitEthernet driver (do_net_recv, do_net_send, do_net_irq_mode) supporting both poll-mode and interrupt-driven packet I/O. Also introduces alloc_page_helper (page-table walk and allocation for mapping a virtual address). The mm.c and sched.c are carried forward from P4 with minor additions; net.c is entirely new. Network DMA buffers require the virtual memory subsystem from P4.

nodes.label: Project 6 — On-Disk File System
nodes.description: Implements a complete on-disk file system above the SD card: superblock and bitmap-based inode/block allocation (mkfs, alloc_inode, alloc_block, free_inode, free_block), directory operations (do_mkdir, do_rmdir, do_ls, do_cd), hard and soft linking (do_link), and POSIX-like file operations (do_touch, do_cat, do_fopen, do_fread, do_fwrite). The mm.c and sched.c are carried forward from P5; disk.c, file.c, and fs.c are entirely new.

edges.label: P2B's preemptive sched.c and lock.c are direct extensions of P2A's cooperative ready-queue design; do_block/do_unblock semantics must be understood before adding priority preemption, timers, and futex
P3's sched.c builds the process lifecycle (do_spawn, do_exit, do_kill, do_waitpid) directly on top of P2B's priority scheduler and max_priority_node ready-queue infrastructure

P4's mm.c and sched.c integrate virtual address spaces into P3's process lifecycle; do_exec and do_exit require the PCB and spawn primitives established in P3 to allocate and tear down page tables per process
P5 carries forward P4's complete mm.c and sched.c, and net.c's kernel DMA buffers depend on alloc_user_page and the SV39 page-table infrastructure implemented in P4
P6 carries forward all of P5's mm.c and sched.c, and the new fs/disk layers use alloc_page_helper (introduced in P5) for kernel buffer mapping during disk block I/O
